summarization.a: This figure presents a conceptual illustration of one complete isotropic thermal ALE half-cycle on θ-Al₂O₃(001) based on DFT insights. HF adsorption forms a fluorinated Al–F surface layer (left), followed by H₂O exposure that facilitates ligand exchange and desorption of volatile AlF₃ species, restoring an Al₂O₃ surface configuration (right).

a.question: Describe the complete sequence of chemical events in this single thermal ALE half-cycle on θ-Al₂O₃(001) as computed by DFT?
a.answer: The half-cycle begins with spontaneous adsorption of 18 HF molecules onto the clean oxygen-terminated θ-Al₂O₃(001) surface, forming a fully fluorinated Al–F top layer while releasing water as a byproduct. In the second step, exposure to 9 H₂O molecules triggers ligand exchange: water replaces fluorine ligands on surface aluminum, dramatically lowering the desorption energy and allowing the entire AlF₃ monolayer to leave as volatile AlF₃(g). This cleanly regenerates the original hydroxylated alumina surface, completing one atomic-layer-precision, isotropic etch half-cycle.

a.question: What fundamental chemical property of the Al–F surface layer makes the second (H₂O) step essential for low-temperature etching?
a.answer: The Al–F bonds formed after HF exposure are extremely strong and thermally stable by themselves, preventing spontaneous desorption of aluminum below ~600–700 °C. Water acts as a ligand that coordinates to surface Al, significantly weakens the Al–surface oxygen bonds, and stabilizes the departing AlF₃ molecule, reducing the desorption barrier to at substantially reduced temperatures compared to thermal desorption. This ligand-promoted etching mechanism is the key property enabling truly thermal, damage-free ALE of alumina at CMOS-compatible temperatures.

a.question: Compared to conventional plasma-based ALE of Al₂O₃, what is the primary advantage of this HF → H₂O sequential thermal process?
a.answer: High isotropy and absence of ion-induced damage due to purely neutral, gas-phase chemistry.

a.question: Is this DFT-proposed HF/H₂O thermal process suitable for conformal atomic-layer etching of Al₂O₃ in high-aspect-ratio 3D nanostructures?
